---
title: "UCLCHEM"
subtitle: "A Gas-Grain Chemical Code for Astrochemistry"
jupyter: uclchem_school
theme: sandstone
fontsize: 16pt
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    incremental: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    highlight-style: github
    slide-number: true
    scrollable: true
    chalkboard: true
    mermaid-format: svg
    include-in-header:
      - text: <script src="incremental-show-first.js"></script>
      - text: <script src="auto-fragment.js"></script>
      - text: "<style>.reveal .fragment { transition: none !important; }</style>"
execute:
  eval: false
  enabled: false
  echo: true
---

## Workshop Overview {.smaller}

| Day | Morning (10:00–12:00) | Afternoon (13:00–15:30) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monday** | Installation & setup *(Gijs)* | **Overview & history** *(Gijs, Serena)* |
| **Tuesday** | Chemistry & ice *(Tobias)* | Collapse & post-processing / NEATH *(Felix)* |
| **Wednesday** | Warm-up physics & radiative treatment *(Tram)* | Shocks, heating, cooling, cosmic rays *(Kasia, Tram, Gijs)* |
| **Thursday** | Advanced modelling *(Marcus)* | Elective / requested topics |
| **Friday** | Free collaboration day | — |

Each afternoon 15:30–17:00: Continued tutorial, open discussion & participant contributions.

Coffee breaks at: 10.45 and at 15:15 

---

## Today's Programme

- **Morning** (10:00–12:00) — Installation & setup
- **Afternoon** (13:00–15:15) — UCLCHEM History, overview and a starter tutorial
- **Discussion** (15:30–17:00) — Open discussion & participant contributions

::: {.callout-note}
During this session we will focus on a birds-eye-view of UCLCHEM. During the coming three days we will discuss everything in more detail.
:::
M

# Part 1 — Motivation

---

## UCLCHEM {style="text-align: center;"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5em; font-size: 1.2em;"}
**A Gas-Grain Chemical Code for Astrochemistry**
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: gray;"}
Holdship et al. 2017, AJ 154, 38
:::

& 

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5em; font-size: 1.2em;"}
**UCLCHEM 4.0: A Modern Open Source Gas-Grain Framework for Astrochemical Modeling.**
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: gray;"}
Vermariën et al. 2026, in prep.
:::


---

## What This Talk Covers

Tue–Thu sessions go deep on each component, guided by dedicated experts.

---

## The Observer's Problem

Telescopes like **ALMA** and **JWST** detect molecular line emission:

- Maps of $\mathrm{CO, CH_3OH, HCN}$, complex organics...
- Line ratios and column densities encode **temperature**, **density**, **cosmic rays**, **history**, 



But to extract physical conditions from observed abundances, you need a **forward model**:

- *Given these physical conditions and chemical network, what abundances do we predict?*
- A chemical model takes **physical conditions** + **chemical network** as input, producing **abundances over time** as output.

---

## The Interstellar Medium: The Chemical Playground

| Environment | Temperature (K) | Density ($\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$) | Key processes |
|---|---|---|---|
| (Collapsing) Dark cloud | 10 K | $10–10^6$ | Freeze-out, CR ionization, ice chemistry |
| Prestellar Core | 100–300 K | $10^6–10^8$ | Slow ice sublimation |
| shocks | 10–4000 K | $10^4$–$10^7$ | Sputtering, rapid sublimation |
| Your model | ??? K | ??? $\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ | ??? | 

Chemistry is **time-dependent**: the abundance of CO today depends on the full history of that parcel of gas.

---

## The Modelling Challenge

1. **Time-dependent**: abundances evolve over $10^4–10^7$ years and can consists of many different *stages*
2. **Multi-phase**: species exist in the gas *and* frozen on grain surfaces
3. **Large network**: 300+ species coupled by 3,000+ reactions
4. **Non-linear and stiff**: every abundance depends on every other and the physics; timescales span many orders of magnitude

It is a large system of differential equations, requiring careful consideration of which numerical solver is used.

---

## What UCLCHEM Gives You

- Time-dependent abundance profiles for any species in the network
- For any (combination of) astrophysical environment (collapse, shock, Prestellar Core, ...)
- Starting from user-defined initial conditions
- Output can be passed into SpectralRadex for line radiative transfer modelling.

# Part 2 — Physical Environments

---

## Physical Parameters: Setting the Stage

All parameters are passed as a Python **dictionary**. Unspecified parameters take sensible defaults for a (dark) cloud context.

| Parameter | Description | Typical value |
|---|---|---|
| `initialDens` | Initial gas density ($\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$) | $10^2–10^6$ |
| `initialTemp` | Initial temperature ($\mathrm{K}$) | $10–300$ |
| `finalTime` | Simulation duration (years) | $10^4–10^7$ |
| `radfield` | UV radiation field (Habing) | $1-10^5$ |
| `zeta` | Cosmic ray ionization rate ($\zeta_{gal}$) | $1-10^5 \times 1.3×10^{-17}$ |
| `baseAv` | Visual extinction at cloud edge | 2 |

---

## The Six Physics Modules

The physics module you pick drives **density** and **temperature** evolution. The chemistry is solved along whatever trajectory you then define.


| Module | Physical scenario |
|---|---|
| **Cloud** | Spherical cloud: static or simple freefall collapse |
| **Collapse** | Gravitational collapse with realistic density profiles |
| **Prestellar Core** | Protostellar envelope: temperature rises with time |
| **C-Shock** | MHD shock with ion-neutral drift |
| **J-Shock** | Jump shock with discontinuous front |
| **Postprocessing** | Post-processing of (hydrodynamical) simulations |


---

## Density and Temperature Evolution

Three illustrative scenarios:

- **Cloud** — static density and temperature
- **Collapse** — density rises as $\dot{n} \propto n^{4/3}$ (freefall), temperature stays cold (~10 K)
- **Prestellar Core** — after collapse, temperature rises as $T = 10 + A\,t^B (r/R)^{1/2}\ \mathrm{K}$, driven by protostellar luminosity (constants $A$, $B$ set by stellar mass)
- **C-shock** — density and temperature spike over the dissipation length, then recover; grain ices are sputtered at the saturation time

::: {.callout-note}
Each physics module is covered in depth by a dedicated session later this week.
:::

---

## 1D Structure and UV Shielding

The cloud is divided into $N$ equal segments. Default: **one point** (appropriate for UV-shielded, homogeneous regions).

Column density accumulated inward (more detail in Tram's talk on Wednesday):
$$N_\mathrm{Tot} = \frac{r_\mathrm{cloud}}{N_\mathrm{points}}\, n(r)$$

Visual extinction:
$$A_V = A_{V,0} + \frac{N_\mathrm{Tot}}{1.6 \times 10^{21}\ \mathrm{cm}^{-2}}$$

UV attenuation at each depth:
$$F_\mathrm{UV} = F_0 \exp(-k\, A_V)$$

Self-shielding of **$\mathrm{H}_2$**, **C**, and **CO** is included. If you need a full PDR treatment, use 3D-PDR instead.

---

## Multi-Stage Modelling

Chemistry has **memory**: the history of a cloud matters. Common examples include:

- Cold collapse → warm-up: ices build up during collapse, then sublimate in the warm-up
- Collapse → shock: the shock chemistry depends on the pre-shock abundances
- Post-processing: the chemistry depends on the full history of density and temperature in the simulation

The final abundances of one stage become the **initial abundances** of the next. You chain them like this:

```python
cold = uclchem.model.Cloud(param_dict={...})
hot  = uclchem.model.HotCore(
    param_dict={...},
    previous_model=cold,
)
```


![](figures/twostage.jpg){width=120%}


# Part 3 — Gas-Phase Chemistry

---

## The Chemical Network

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Gas-phase reactions | 2,000 (UMIST22 default) |
| Grain surface reactions | 800+ |
| Total species | 300+ |
| Grain species (surface + bulk) | ~200 |

Species notation at a glance:

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `CO`, `H2O` | Gas phase |
| `#CO`, `#H2O` | Grain surface |
| `@CO`, `@H2O` | Bulk ice |
| `$CO` | Total ice = surface + bulk |

---

## Gas-Phase Reaction Types

| Type | Example | Rate form |
|---|---|---|
| Photodissociation | `$\mathrm{CO + h\nu → C + O}$` | $\alpha F_\mathrm{UV} \exp(-k A_V)$ |
| Cosmic ray ionization | `$\mathrm{H_2 + CRP → H_2^+ + e^-}$` | $\alpha\, \zeta$ |
| CR-induced photons | secondary UV | $\alpha (T/300)^\beta\, \zeta$ |
| Two-body collision | `$\mathrm{H + OH → H_2O}$` | $k\, X_i X_j n_H$ |
| Ion-neutral | `$\mathrm{H_3^+ + CO → HCO^+ + H_2}$` | $k\, X_i X_j n_H$ |

---

## Rate Coefficients: The Modified Arrhenius Equation

For the gas-phase reactions:

$$k = \alpha \left(\frac{T}{300\ \mathrm{K}}\right)^\beta \exp\!\left(-\frac{\gamma}{T}\right)$$

- $\alpha$: pre-exponential factor
- $\beta$: temperature power-law index
- $\gamma$: activation barrier (K)

Other reaction parameterizations:

- UV: $k = \alpha\, F_\mathrm{UV}\, \exp(-k\, A_V)$
- Cosmic ray: $k = \alpha\, \zeta$   where $\zeta$ is in units of $1.3 \times 10^{-17}\ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$

Temperature ranges ($T_\mathrm{min}$, $T_\mathrm{max}$) from laboratory measurements; extrapolation is supported and recommended.

---

## UMIST vs. KIDA

Both are well established gas phase chemical databases:

| Database | Reference | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **UMIST22** | Millar et al 2024 (A&A 682, A109) | Default in UCLCHEM 4.0|
| **KIDA24** | Wakelam et al. 2024 (A&A 689, A63 ) | Supported but not well verified yet |

The **same Fortran code** runs either network. The choice is made during the MakeRates and compilation step.

---

## The ODE System for Gas Chemistry

For each species $i$, the fractional abundance $X_i = n_i / n_{\mathrm{H},nuclei}$ evolves as:

$$\frac{dX_i}{dt} = \sum_{j,k} k_{jk}\, X_j X_k\, n_\mathrm{H} + \sum_j k_j\, X_j - \sum_k k_{ik}\, X_k\, n_\mathrm{H}\, X_i - \sum_l k_l\, X_i$$

~300 species → **300 + 2 ($dn,dT$) coupled non-linear ODEs**

The system is **stiff**: some reactions happen on timescales of seconds (surface diffusion), others on timescales of millions of years (slow gas-phase reactions). Explicit integration methods would be impossibly slow.

---

# Part 4 — Gas-Grain Chemistry

---

## Dust Grains: Why They Matter

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="75%"}
- At cold temperatures (< ~100 K): molecules collide with grains and **stick** onto the surface. This is freeze-out.
- The grain surface acts as a catalyst, enabling formation of more complex molecules on the surface.

Classic example — **methanol formation**:
$$\text{CO(gas)} \xrightarrow{\text{freeze}} \text{\#CO} \xrightarrow{+\text{\#H}} \text{\#HCO} \xrightarrow{+\text{\#H}} \text{\#H}_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{+\text{\#H}} \text{\#CH}_3\text{O} \xrightarrow{+\text{\#H}} \text{CH}_3\text{OH(gas)}$$

Without grain surfaces, methanol formation in the ISM would be impossible.
:::

::: {.column width="25%"}
![Cuppen et al. 2017](figures/gas_grain_.svg)
:::
:::

---

## Freeze-Out: Gas → Grain Surface

$$k_\mathrm{freeze} = \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{\beta \cdot 1.671 \times 10^{-3}}{r_g \, T_\mathrm{gas}}\right)}_{\text{ion correction}} \times \alpha \times v_\mathrm{th}\sqrt{\frac{T_\mathrm{gas}}{m}} \times \frac{\sigma_g}{n_\mathrm{H}}$$

where $\beta$ is the ion charge (0 for neutrals), $\alpha$ the sticking coefficient, and $\sigma_g/n_\mathrm{H}$ the grain cross-section per H nucleus.

Switched off when $T_\mathrm{dust} > T_\mathrm{max}$ or desorption rate $>$ freeze-out rate.

---

## Surface Reaction Mechanisms (More by Tobias Tomorrow)

- **Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH)** — thermal diffusion: both species hop between binding sites until they meet. Stops above ~150 K. Example: `#H + #H → #$\mathrm{H}_2$`
- **Eley-Rideal (ER)** — direct impact: a gas-phase species lands directly on an adsorbed reactant. No diffusion required. Example: `H(gas) + #CO → #HCO`
- Both can produce **gas-phase** or **surface** products; branching ratio set by reaction exothermicity (Minissale et al. 2016)



---

## Desorption: Surface to Gas

Four mechanisms return species to the gas phase:

| Mechanism | Rate | Dominant where |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | $k = \nu_0 \exp(-E_B / T)$ | Warm regions, Prestellar Cores |
| **Cosmic ray** | CR impact heats grain transiently | Dense, UV-shielded clouds |
| **Photodesorption** | UV photon ejects surface molecule | PDR surfaces, cloud edges |
| **Chemical** | Exothermic surface reaction releases product | Everywhere |

- Master switch: `desorb=True/False`
- Individual switches: `h2desorb`, `crdesorb`, `uvdesorb`

---

## The Three-Phase Model
Reminder about notation:

| Phase | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Gas | `CO` | Free molecules |
| Surface | `#CO` | Adsorbed outer monolayer |
| Bulk ice | `@CO` | Buried in the ice mantle |
| Total ice | `$CO` | `#CO` + `@CO` |

**Surface $\leftrightarrow$ bulk transfer** via:

- **Geometric**: as new ice layers grow, old surface becomes bulk; as ice erodes, bulk is exposed
- **Swapping**: individual molecules can swap between surface and bulk

---

# Part 5 — The UCLCHEM Framework

---

## Architecture: Python + Fortran

```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
%%| echo: false
flowchart LR
    A["User<br/>param dict"] --> B["Python API<br/>uclchem.model.*"]
    B --> C["Fortran Core<br/>ODE integrator<br/>+ rate calculator"]
    C --> D["Results<br/>DataFrame / CSV"]
    E["MakeRates<br/>species + reactions"] --> F["network.f90<br/>nodes.f90"]
    F --> G["pip install .<br/>compile Fortran"]
    G --> C
```

- **Python**: model classes, analysis tools, and parameter handling. All user-facing.
- **Fortran**: ODE integration and reaction rate calculation, with the network **hard-coded** for speed.
- Each UCLCHEM installation contains exactly **one chemical network**.
- It cannot be distributed as a pre-built wheel and must be compiled locally.

---

## Explicit vs. Implicit Integration

**Explicit (Forward) Euler** — uses the current state to step forward:
$$X^{n+1} = X^n + \Delta t\, f(X^n)$$

**Implicit (Backward) Euler** — uses the next state:
$$X^{n+1} = X^n + \Delta t\, f(X^{n+1})$$

The implicit form requires solving a nonlinear system at each step — but that cost is worth it.

**Stability consequences for stiff chemistry:**

| Method | Stability | Step size constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Explicit | Conditionally stable | $\Delta t < 2/\|\lambda_\mathrm{max}\|$ |
| Implicit | Unconditionally stable | No constraint |

The fastest eigenvalue in our network ($\lambda_\mathrm{max}$) comes from fast surface reactions. An explicit solver is forced into millisecond steps. An implicit solver can take steps of years.



![Press et al. 1986](figures/stiffness.png)
---

## DVODE: The Solver Behind UCLCHEM

UCLCHEM uses **DVODE** (Brown, Byrne & Hindmarsh 1989), a workhorse of stiff chemistry solvers.

- Implements **Backward Differentiation Formulas (BDF)** — variable order 1–5
- **Variable stepsize**: adapts $\Delta t$ to the current stiffness of the network
- **Variable order**: higher order = fewer steps, more memory per step. DVODE picks the most efficient combination.

Two tolerances control solution quality:

| Parameter | Role | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `reltol` | Relative error per step | ~$10^{-8}$ |
| `abstol_factor` | Absolute error floor (for near-zero abundances) | ~$10^{-20}$ |
| `abstol_min` | Absolute error floor (for all abundances) | ~$10^{-20}$ |

Error codes: `0` = success; negative values = failure.

We always validate after running a model:

```python
model.check_error()
model.check_conservation(element_list=["H", "C", "N", "O", "S"])
```

::: {.callout-important}
If element conservation is > 1%, tighten tolerances: `"reltol": 1e-12, "abstol_factor": 1e-20, "abstol_min": 1e-25`
:::

---

## The Main Integration Loop

```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
%%| echo: false
flowchart TD
    A["Set parameters<br/>defaults → user overrides"] --> B["Initialise physics<br/>& chemical abundances"]
    B --> C{"time < finalTime<br/>OR density < finalDens?"}
    C -->|yes| D["Calculate next<br/>target time"]
    D --> E["DVODE: integrate<br/>t → t_target"]
    E --> J{"ISTATE"}
    J -->|"2 success"| F["Update physics<br/>variables"]
    J -->|"-1 max steps<br/>-4/-5 step failure"| K["Reduce t_target × 0.1"]
    J -->|"-2 tolerances<br/>too small"| L["Relax abstol,<br/>reltol × 10"]
    J -->|"-3 invalid inputs"| M["Abort"]
    K -->|"retries < 10"| E
    K -->|"retries >= 10"| M
    L -->|"retries < 10"| E
    L -->|"retries >= 10"| M
    F --> G["Apply instantaneous<br/>physics (e.g. sputtering)"]
    G --> H["Write output"]
    H --> C
    C -->|no| I["Optionally save<br/>final abundances"]
```

---

## MakeRates: Customising the Network

MakeRates is a **preprocessing tool** — generates the Fortran source compiled into UCLCHEM.

```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
%%| echo: false
flowchart LR
    A["species.csv <br/> (mass, binding energy,<br/>formation enthalpy)"] --> C["MakeRates.py"]
    B["reactions.csv <br/> (UMIST / KIDA / UCL format)"] --> C
    C --> D["network.f90<br/> odes.f90 <br/> f2py_constants.f90 <br/> constants.py"]
    D --> E["pip install .<br/>recompile"]
```

**When to use it:**

- Changing chemical network (e.g. UMIST → KIDA)
- Custom ice chemistry
- Adding new species or reactions

**Workflow**:
```bash
cd Makerates && python MakeRates.py    # edit user_settings.yaml first
cd .. && pip install .                 # always recompile after
```

::: {.callout-note}
For this school we use the default UMIST22-based network. MakeRates is for advanced customisation.
:::

---

## Outputs and Analysis Tools

**File outputs (legacy) ** (controlled via parameters):

| Parameter | Content |
|---|---|
| `outputFile` | All species at all timesteps |
| `columnFile` | Selected species (`out_species`) only |
| `ratesFile` | Individual reaction rates |
| `abundSaveFile` / `abundLoadFile` | Persist abundances between stages |

**Python analysis API**:

```python
df  = model.get_dataframes()          # pandas DataFrames
fig = model.create_abundance_plot(species=["CO", "$CO"])
model.check_conservation(["H", "C", "N", "O", "S"])
```

---

# Part 6 — Practical Preview

---

## Running Your First Model

```python
import uclchem

params = {
    "initialDens": 1e4,   # cm^-3
    "initialTemp": 10.0,  # K
    "finalTime":   1e6,   # years
    "freefall":    False,
}

cloud = uclchem.model.Cloud(
    param_dict=params,
)

cloud.check_error()
cloud.check_conservation(element_list=["H", "C", "N", "O", "S"])

fig, ax = cloud.create_abundance_plot(
    species=["CO", "$CO", "H2O", "$H2O", "CH3OH", "$CH3OH"]
)
```

---

## Multi-Stage Workflow Example

```python
# Stage 1: cold dark cloud collapse — build up ices
cold = uclchem.model.Cloud(
    param_dict={
        "initialDens": 1e4, "finalDens": 5e6,
        "freefall": True,   "finalTime": 3e4,
    }
)
cold.check_error()

# Stage 2: Prestellar Core warm-up — sublimate ices
hot = uclchem.model.PrestellarCore(
    param_dict={
        "initialDens": 1e6, "finalTime": 1e6,
        "baseAv": 20,
    },
    previous_model=cold,
)
hot.check_error()
```

The molecules that build up on the grains as the cold gas collapses are released back into the gas phase in the warm-up phase.

---

## What's Coming This Week {.smaller}

| Day | Morning (10:00–12:00) | Afternoon (13:00–15:30) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monday** | Installation & setup *(Gijs)* | **Overview & history** *(Gijs, Serena)* |
| **Tuesday** | Chemistry & ice *(Tobias)* | Collapse & post-processing / NEATH *(Felix)* |
| **Wednesday** | Warm-up physics & radiative treatment *(Tram)* | Shocks, heating, cooling, cosmic rays *(Kasia, Tram, Gijs)* |
| **Thursday** | Advanced modelling *(Marcus)* | Elective / requested topics |
| **Friday** | Free collaboration day | — |

Each afternoon 15:30–17:00: Continued tutorial, open discussion & participant contributions.

Coffee breaks at: 10.45 and at 15:15 


Version: **v4.0.0-rc3** — full documentation at [uclchem.github.io](https://uclchem.github.io)
